exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage:
When you are lucky enough to (1) ...... tickets to a major music festival, it is worth knowing the unwritten rules of audience behaviour. In many countries, it is perfectly normal to comment (2) ...... the performance as it is happening. You might hear someone lean over to a friend and say, 'This is incredible. I would come back (3) ...... this band any time!'

In other settings, however, such as a classical concert hall, people tend not (4) ...... make any noise at all while the music is playing. This is seen as a form (5) ...... respect for the performers. Clapping between movements, for example, might be regarded (6) ...... impolite by more traditional audience members. It is expected that you show your enthusiasm (7) ...... applause only at the very end.

If you are unsure what to do, a useful tip is to wait and (8) ...... what the people around you are doing. If you remain uncertain (9) ...... the right moment to respond, simply follow the lead of those with more experience. The most important thing is (10) ...... you enjoy the occasion, whatever the setting!

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: Grandma never puts sugar in her tea, but she does use an artificial (SWEET) ...... sometimes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from SWEET, so the correct answer is "sweetener".

wrongOptions.sweet: The root SWEET cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "sweetener".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SWEET that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SWEET → sweetener: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "sweetener" is the word-formation answer from the root SWEET in this context.

items.sentence: I can't believe you actually tried those deep-fried insects. That sounds completely (DISGUST) ......!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective describing food, so the correct answer is "disgusting".

wrongOptions.disgust: The root DISGUST cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "disgusting".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of DISGUST that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: DISGUST → disgusting: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "disgusting" is the word-formation answer from the root DISGUST in this context.

items.sentence: The students waited (ANXIOUS) ...... outside the hall as the exam results were about to be announced.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb modifying waited, so the correct answer is "anxiously".

wrongOptions.anxious: The root ANXIOUS cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "anxiously".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ANXIOUS that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ANXIOUS → anxiously: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "anxiously" is the word-formation answer from the root ANXIOUS in this context.

items.sentence: Did you know that the potato (ORIGIN) ...... in the Andes mountains of South America?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past verb from ORIGIN, so the correct answer is "originated".

wrongOptions.origin: The root ORIGIN cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "originated".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ORIGIN that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ORIGIN → originated: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "originated" is the word-formation answer from the root ORIGIN in this context.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from CREATE, so the correct answer is "creative".

wrongOptions.create: The root CREATE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "creative".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of CREATE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: CREATE → creative: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "creative" is the word-formation answer from the root CREATE in this context.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from CONTAIN, so the correct answer is "container".

wrongOptions.contain: The root CONTAIN cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "container".

explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
You have to stir the paint thoroughly before you use it. **stirred**
The paint ...... thoroughly before you use it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is obligation + passive, so the correct answer is "has to be stirred".

wrongOptions.to be stirred: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has to be stirred", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
A baker brought the birthday cake to our door this morning. **had**
We ...... a baker this morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is causative had, so the correct answer is "had the cake delivered by".

wrongOptions.the cake delivered by: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had the cake delivered by", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Elaine tasted my pasta dish to check if it needed more seasoning. **got**
I ...... my pasta dish to check if it needed more seasoning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is get someone to do something, so the correct answer is "got Elaine to taste".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "got Elaine to taste", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Many people say that dark chocolate is good for your mood. **said**
Dark chocolate ...... good for your mood.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive reporting, so the correct answer is "is said to be".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is said to be", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Some experts have claimed that green tea can help prevent certain illnesses. **been**
It ...... that green tea can help prevent certain illnesses.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present perfect passive, so the correct answer is "has been said".

wrongOptions.been said: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
They've been building that new sports stadium for over a year. **construction**
That new sports stadium ...... for over a year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ongoing project, so the correct answer is "has been under construction".

wrongOptions.been under construction: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has been under construction", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
These herbs came from a greenhouse nearby. **grown**
These herbs ...... a greenhouse nearby.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past passive, so the correct answer is "were grown in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "were grown in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Why not ask an electrician to fit your new cooker? **get**
Why don't you ...... an electrician?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is causative get, so the correct answer is "get your cooker fitted by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get your cooker fitted by", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I think this soup needs more herbs. **lacking**
I think this soup ...... herbs.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "is lacking in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is lacking in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I think this yoghurt has gone **D** off, so we should throw it out.
items.answer: D – off, so we should throw it out.
acceptedAnswers: D – off, so we should throw it out.

items.sentence: I ran **F** into our old neighbour at the market this morning.
items.answer: F – into our old neighbour at the market this morning.
acceptedAnswers: F – into our old neighbour at the market this morning.
items.explanation: Meet by chance → ran **into** our old neighbour.

items.sentence: We've run out **B** of butter, so could you buy some more?
items.answer: B – of butter, so could you buy some more?
acceptedAnswers: B – of butter, so could you buy some more?
items.explanation: No more left → run out **of** butter.

items.sentence: Dad is trying **A** out a completely new recipe this evening.
items.answer: A – out a completely new recipe this evening.
acceptedAnswers: A – out a completely new recipe this evening.
items.explanation: Test something new → trying **out** a new recipe.

items.sentence: It must be getting **C** on for lunchtime already!
items.explanation: Approaching a time → getting **on** for lunchtime.

items.sentence: You must come **G** round for a meal one evening.
items.answer: G – round for a meal one evening.
acceptedAnswers: G – round for a meal one evening.
items.explanation: Visit someone's home → come **round** for a meal.

items.sentence: This pie hasn't turned **E** out quite as well as I hoped.
items.answer: E – out quite as well as I hoped.
acceptedAnswers: E – out quite as well as I hoped.
items.explanation: Result of cooking → hasn't turned **out** quite as well.

items.sentenceAfter: in unexpectedly last night, so I had to cook something quickly.

wrongOptions.A: "turned" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.turned: "turned" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.C: "came" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When you see a finished past-time expression, reach for the past simple first.
